# Questions in Livestream 25

Live Stream #25: The Canonical Age For Levitical Priests

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/au5AYPIFPbY?t=246" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:06</a> | Does he know of anywhere to study Syriac and the spread of Christianity along the Silk Road? | He called it a worthwhile topic but said it is outside his field and he could not responsibly recommend programs. | He said the spread of Christianity along the Silk Road is a valuable and under-studied topic, but Syriac studies and New Testament-related work are outside his Old Testament specialty. He had looked at his own Syriac books before answering, yet the scholars he knew from those resources were mostly retired or deceased, so he could not assess the current field or recommend programs responsibly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/au5AYPIFPbY?t=367" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">6:07</a> | Did Egyptians think the Milky Way was the heavenly Nile? | No. He said the celestial Nile was conceived differently, while the Milky Way was identified with the body of the sky goddess Nut. | He distinguished the celestial counterpart to the Nile from the Milky Way, saying Egyptians understood that river-like counterpart as traveling under the earth rather than across the sky. The Milky Way, in his summary of Egyptian cosmology, was associated with Nut, the sky goddess, whose body arched over the earth god Geb. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/au5AYPIFPbY?t=490" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">8:10</a> | What does he think of Titus Kennedy's work on evidence for the Exodus? | He was strongly negative and said Kennedy mishandles dates and archaeology. | He criticized Kennedy's method as driven by a particular early-Exodus perspective and said he sees Kennedy pulling items in and out of history as evidence even when the dates do not match. His example was the handling of the Ipuwer Papyrus, which he described as Middle Kingdom in language and culture but wrongly late-dated to fit an Exodus argument. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/au5AYPIFPbY?t=634" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:34</a> | What are his thoughts on revisionist claims that the period around 1100-900 BC is overstretched? | He rejected the claim and said conventional chronology is better supported by synchronisms with Assyria, Egypt, and surrounding nations. | He said a great deal can happen in a short historical period and that revisionist chronological claims depend on suppositions he does not think hold up. In his view, once synchronisms from Assyria, Egypt, and neighboring nations are weighed, conventional chronology has plainly better evidence than the revisionist alternatives. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/au5AYPIFPbY?t=752" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:32</a> | Is Herod's slaughter of the infants a historical parallel to Pharaoh's killing of Hebrew sons, and should we expect physical evidence? | Yes, he saw it as an Exodus parallel, but not one likely to leave identifiable archaeological remains. | He treated Herod's act as part of a larger reenactment of Exodus themes in the life of Christ, with a threatened ruler killing infants much as Pharaoh did. He also said such massacres were common enough in ancient history that they might not be independently recorded, and even if infant remains or ossuaries survived, they likely could not be tied specifically to Herod's massacre. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/au5AYPIFPbY?t=988" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:28</a> | Did Egyptians have dragons, and if not, why not? | He said Egypt had dragon-like demon imagery, but crocodiles, cobras, and other river creatures filled many of the roles dragons fill elsewhere. | He said Egyptians did have demonic figures that could look dragon-like, such as snakes with feet and wings, but they were demons rather than natural dragon creatures. Egypt's own environment supplied powerful reptile imagery through cobras, Nile monitors, crocodiles, and serpent figures such as Apophis, so Egyptian myth did not need dragons in the same role found in Babylonian, Carthaginian, or European material. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/au5AYPIFPbY?t=1188" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:48</a> | Does he agree that Deuteronomy's structure supports an early date because it resembles Hittite suzerainty treaties? | Yes. He said Kitchen's treaty-structure argument is complicated but basically sound. | He agreed with Kenneth Kitchen's argument and described it as a large, detailed comparison of ancient Near Eastern treaties, laws, and covenants. Kitchen's case, as he summarized it, compares treaty structures such as invocations, promises, punishments, terms, stipulations, and epilogues, then observes that Deuteronomy's structure most closely resembles Hittite suzerainty treaties, making it one argument among several for an early composition of the Torah. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/au5AYPIFPbY?t=1492" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:52</a> | Is the name Potiphar anachronistic for Joseph's period? | No. He said it is a genuine Egyptian name type and fits the broader naming patterns well enough. | He said Potiphar and Potiphera are essentially the same Egyptian name type and connected it with a name ending in Re. Since he places Joseph in the Second Intermediate Period and says Re-names are attested from the Old Kingdom onward and remain popular for a long span, he saw Potiphar as a good biblical example of an Egyptian name rather than an anachronism. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/au5AYPIFPbY?t=1713" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:33</a> | Did female virginity before marriage matter much in ancient Egypt? | Not in the way it did in ancient Israel. He described Egyptian sexual norms as comparatively permissive. | He contrasted Egypt with Israel, saying premarital sex created serious stigma for women in Israel but does not appear to have mattered nearly as much in Egypt. He described Egyptian society as having premarital sex, prostitution, sex outside marriage, frequent marriage and divorce, and festival contexts where such behavior could occur before or even after marriage. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/au5AYPIFPbY?t=1851" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:51</a> | Did Israelites take eschatology and resurrection beliefs from Persia? | He said ancient religions shared and adapted ideas, so Israel could draw from surrounding cultures without that undermining inspiration or infallibility. | He answered that ancient Israelite religion existed within a broad ancient Near Eastern pool of ideas involving Egypt, Canaan, Tyre and Sidon, Philistia, Persia, Babylon, Assyria, Media, and Elam. He allowed that Israelites in exilic and post-exilic contexts could borrow and adapt concepts for Yahweh worship, but argued that using cultural sources to anchor teaching in the real world does not damage inspiration, infallibility, or inerrancy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/au5AYPIFPbY?t=2182" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">36:22</a> | Is there really a contradiction over Moses's father-in-law being called Jethro or Reuel? | He said no. Multiple names within tribal societies, or different family relationships across Moses's marriages, are enough to keep it from being a real contradiction. | He offered two possible explanations: in tribal groups, one person can have more than one name, and Moses himself likely had both Hebrew and Egyptian forms of his name; alternatively, the names could relate to different family connections through Moses's Midianite and Cushite marriages. Because more than one plausible explanation exists, he said the text should not be treated as a genuine contradiction even if the exact harmonization is uncertain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/au5AYPIFPbY?t=2390" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">39:50</a> | Was there a real Tower of Babel? | Yes, but he framed it as a real ziggurat-like place for man-centered worship rather than a fantastically tall tower reaching outer space. | He said Babel should not be imagined as a tower literally reaching outer space, but as a ziggurat-like mound or platform for humans to approach heaven on their own terms. The offense, in his reading, was man-centered religion: people refusing to be scattered and attempting to worship Yahweh in a self-prescribed way, which he compared to later high-place worship condemned in Kings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/au5AYPIFPbY?t=2660" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">44:20</a> | Should `raqia` be translated as dome instead of expanse or heaven? | He said "expanse" or "celestial ocean" are better, while "dome" and "heavens" are nearer the edges of the term's meaning. | He said older scholarship often connected `raqia` with the celestial ocean, which he thought may be the truest translation, while the most basic sense is an expanse. He was cautious about both strict concordism and accommodationist approaches, preferring a natural-language middle ground and deferring the technical translation issues to specialists such as Paul Lawrence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/au5AYPIFPbY?t=2997" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">49:57</a> | What are his thoughts on the Kregel Exegetical Library and Duane Garrett's Exodus commentary? | He had not read Garrett's commentary and warned that commentary quality varies book by book, so no series should be treated as uniformly best. | He said he did not know much about the Kregel Exegetical Library and had not read Garrett's Exodus commentary. His broader counsel was that commentary series vary sharply by individual volume; he tends to consult different series depending on the book and does not assume any one series is consistently best. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/au5AYPIFPbY?t=3146" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:26</a> | Is Genesis 10:10-11 referring to the Uruk expansion? | He regarded that identification as possible but too weakly evidenced to adopt confidently. | After reading Genesis 10:10-11, he said the Nimrod and Uruk-expansion question is hard to pin down because the period leaves scholars with a deficit of information. He acknowledged evidence for some expansion during the Uruk dynasties, but said the timing, extent, and connection to Babel are too uncertain, and he is uncomfortable adopting a theory without stronger evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/au5AYPIFPbY?t=3402" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">56:42</a> | In what ways did Sumerian culture affect the writing of Genesis? | Very little directly by the time Genesis was written down, in his view, because Sumerian culture was already long gone except perhaps in distant landmarks and names. | He said Sumerian culture belonged to a much earlier Bronze Age setting, while he places the written finalization of Genesis around the Late Bronze Age, roughly the thirteenth century BC. By then, in his view, Sumerian culture was long gone except for residual landmarks, city references, or names, so Genesis would show little direct Sumerian cultural influence despite preserving older traditions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/au5AYPIFPbY?t=3678" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:01:18</a> | How did snake charming work in the ancient Near East, and where did it come from? | He said it is attested across the Near East and seems to have worked broadly like later snake charming, with performance, handling, and family transmission of the craft. | He said ancient sources attest snake charming from Egypt to Babylon, and he described it as remarkably similar to modern practice. Although snakes cannot hear music in the relevant sense, performers used movement, distraction, handling, baskets, and probably family transmission of the craft; he suggested entertainment and religious practice as likely contexts. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/au5AYPIFPbY?t=3929" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:05:29</a> | Did ancient Near Eastern peoples have eschatologies, and do pre-exilic Hebrew texts show one? | Yes. He distinguished personal from final eschatology and said both appear in Hebrew texts, though final eschatology becomes clearer later. | He divided eschatology into personal eschatology, meaning what happens to a person after death, and final eschatology, meaning what happens at the end of days. He said pre-exilic Hebrew texts do show eschatological ideas, such as Sheol and hints in Ecclesiastes and the prophets, while post-exilic texts contain more developed forms, especially for personal eschatology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/au5AYPIFPbY?t=4193" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:09:53</a> | What are his thoughts on the recently announced tomb of Ramesses II's treasurer? | He was waiting for publication, but said early reports and photos suggested a well-preserved tomb with potentially valuable evidence. | He had seen only a few pictures and was still waiting for publication, but he said reports suggested the tomb, paint, plastered walls, writing, and funerary statuary were in good condition. He was especially interested in whether the tomb has a biography section, since that could provide substantial evidence once properly published. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/au5AYPIFPbY?t=4297" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:11:37</a> | What is his preferred commentary on Exodus? | He said he does not have one preferred Exodus commentary, normally uses several, and might choose NICOT if forced to pick. | He said he relies more on peer-reviewed journals and multiple commentaries than on one preferred Exodus volume. When preparing sermons, he tries to consult at least five commentaries; if forced to choose one for Exodus, he might pick the NICOT volume because he regards the NICOT Old Testament standards as high. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/au5AYPIFPbY?t=4616" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:16:56</a> | Is "Mystery Babylon" in Revelation using Ishtar-like imagery? | Partly, but he pushed the reading toward the wider idea of apostate religion rather than a simple goddess identification. | He said the imagery can make a reader think of Babylon, but Revelation 17's "mystery" language should also be read through the idea of a wondrous or paradoxical corporate reality. In his interpretation, Mystery Babylon represents apostate religion or apostate people who fall away from faithful worship, not merely a direct identification with Ishtar imagery. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/au5AYPIFPbY?t=5001" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:23:21</a> | Has he personally had a supernatural encounter, and what can be classified as supernatural? | He said yes, he has seen spirit beings, but he was not ready to discuss the details. | He answered yes, saying he has had supernatural encounters, but he deliberately declined to give details. The only classification he was willing to state was that his experiences were not mainly dreams; he described them as seeing entities or spirit beings and then left the subject there. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/au5AYPIFPbY?t=5104" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:25:04</a> | Is there Egyptian significance to the long ages in Genesis 5? | He noted Egyptian king lists give very long reigns to gods, but argued Genesis 5 is better understood as clan ages rather than individual lifespans. | He acknowledged Egyptian king lists with divine rulers assigned extremely long reigns, but said Genesis 5's numbers should not be read the same way. His preferred explanation is that Genesis 5 and 11 preserve clan ages: the lifespan of a close family unit across generations, with honorific ordering and clan succession explaining the formulaic father-son language. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/au5AYPIFPbY?t=5415" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:30:15</a> | Which film best depicts ancient Egypt? | He did not name a definitive best film, but said Cecil B. DeMille's The Ten Commandments includes many surprisingly accurate Egyptian details despite imperfections. | He said he does not watch many films and could name many poor ancient Egypt films, but he singled out Cecil B. DeMille's The Ten Commandments as surprisingly strong in its Egyptian details. He still had complaints, especially about costuming, but thought the film made a serious and often successful effort to portray Egypt well. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/au5AYPIFPbY?t=5530" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:32:10</a> | Would Egyptian and other ancient idol worshipers have felt tension between divine superiority and human manipulation of the gods? | Yes, but differently by culture. He said Egyptians tried to please dangerous gods, while Canaanites more readily treated idols as leverage points for control. | He explained that ancient Near Eastern gods were often thought to be in one place at one time, so an idol could function as a way of localizing or trapping a deity. Egyptians, in his summary, focused on pleasing potentially dangerous gods through proper offerings and purity, while Canaanite practice could lean more toward extorting the god through the idol, though he qualified that this contrast is simplified and both reverence and manipulation could appear. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/au5AYPIFPbY?t=6147" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:42:27</a> | Is Papyrus Anastasi I evidence for the Anakim or giant traditions? | He thought it gives some support, since its description of unusually tall Shasu helps explain why giant reports in Canaan could have had a real basis. | He said Papyrus Anastasi I refers to Shasu as four to five cubits tall; using the standard eighteen-inch cubit, that would put them around six to seven and a half feet. Because these tall Shasu are roughly contemporary with the biblical setting and located in southern regions the Israelites would pass near, he thought the text gives warrant for reports of giants or Anakim-like traditions, while still allowing some uncertainty. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/au5AYPIFPbY?t=6361" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:46:01</a> | Did Egypt have a concept similar to ghosts? | Yes. He said Egyptians expected regular interaction with the dead, including dream visitations and communication with ancestors. | He said Egyptians absolutely believed in ghosts, along with gods, minor spirits, and demons, and described their worldview as having one foot in this world and one in the spirit world. Egyptian literature, in his summary, includes fairly frequent talk of spectral visitations, the dead entering dreams, and signs or communications from ancestors. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/au5AYPIFPbY?t=6515" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:48:35</a> | If scripture contains numerical problems, how can it still be called infallible? | He limited infallibility to what scripture actually asserts and to the original writings, and said ancient number usage is often idiomatic or textually unstable rather than simply "error." | He said infallibility concerns what Scripture asserts in its own context and, more specifically, the original writings rather than every received textual form. He also argued that ancient numbers can have broader semantic ranges, idiomatic uses such as forty for a long period, textual variants, and culturally different conventions for population counts, so apparent numerical problems need not mean Scripture is asserting a modern-style accounting error. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/au5AYPIFPbY?t=7072" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:57:52</a> | Does God implicitly condemn space exploration and settlement? | He said there is no sweeping biblical prohibition, though some theologians have argued from the earth/heavens distinction and practical settlement would be difficult. | He said the question is legitimate because some theologians have reasoned from texts that assign earth to humanity and the heavens to God. He did not think Scripture gives a sweeping prohibition, but he also said space settlement may be practically difficult because exoplanet research highlights how unusual Earth is and how few known planets seem remotely habitable. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/au5AYPIFPbY?t=7327" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:02:07</a> | Has he ever played the ancient Egyptian game Senet? | No, at least not by rules he would treat as ancient; the boards survive, but the original rulebook does not and modern rules are reconstructed guesses. | He said many Senet boards survive, but no ancient rulebook does. Modern rule sets, in his account, were reconstructed by enthusiasts rather than preserved from Egyptian writings, so although people still make and play Senet boards, he would not treat those rules as genuinely ancient beyond the board and pieces. |
